title_en: Ad-Dardiri and Al-Sharh al-Kabir: Maliki Authority in Egypt
content_en:
Ahmad ibn Muhammad al-'Adawi ad-Dardiri (1127–1201 AH / 1715–1786 CE) was among the most distinguished Maliki scholars of Ottoman Egypt. Born in Bani 'Adi in Upper Egypt, he moved to Cairo where he studied at the great al-Azhar mosque, rose through its scholarly ranks, and ultimately became the chief teacher (shaykh) of the Maliki scholars there. He was known not only for his legal scholarship but for his piety and his affiliation with the Khalwatiyyah Sufi order — a combination of qualities that gave him authority in both the exoteric sciences of fiqh and the inner dimensions of Islamic spiritual practice.

Ad-Dardiri's Al-Sharh al-Kabir ala Mukhtasar Khalil (The Great Commentary on the Summary of Khalil) is his magnum opus in fiqh. It is a detailed commentary on the single most important text in the Maliki tradition: the Mukhtasar of Khalil ibn Ishaq al-Jundi (d. 776 AH / 1374 CE), a compressed summary of Maliki law so authoritative that it became the foundational reference for Maliki fatwa and judicial decisions across North Africa, West Africa, and Egypt for centuries.

The Mukhtasar Khalil is itself a notoriously dense text — deliberately so, as it is designed to pack an enormous quantity of legal information into a compact form suitable for memorization by advanced students. It assumes familiarity with the Maliki tradition and is unintelligible without commentary. Ad-Dardiri's Al-Sharh al-Kabir fills this role, providing the explanations, qualifications, and contextual understanding necessary to make the text usable.

The Maliki school, founded by Imam Malik ibn Anas (93–179 AH / 711–795 CE) of Medina, is one of the four accepted schools of Sunni jurisprudence. Its distinctive methodological features include: a high regard for the practice of the people of Medina ('amal ahl al-Madinah) as evidence of the living Sunnah transmitted through community practice; a willingness to use maslaha mursalah (unrestricted public interest) as a source of law; the doctrine of sadd adh-dhara'i (blocking the means to harm); and considerable flexibility in the application of custom ('urf) as a factor in legal rulings.

Egypt was historically a stronghold of both the Shafi'i and Maliki schools, and ad-Dardiri's career at al-Azhar reflects the Maliki school's continued prominence in Egyptian scholarly life during the Ottoman period. His commentary on Mukhtasar Khalil became one of the most consulted references in Egyptian and North African Maliki courts, used by judges (qadis) and muftis to resolve disputes and issue religious opinions.

Al-Sharh al-Kabir exists in a complementary relationship with the equally famous Hashiyat al-Dasuqi ala ash-Sharh al-Kabir — a supercommentary written by ad-Dardiri's student Muhammad ibn Ahmad al-Dasuqi. The two works together form an inseparable scholarly unit: virtually every serious student of Maliki fiqh reads them together, as al-Dasuqi's notes clarify, expand, and occasionally correct ad-Dardiri's positions. This Dardiri-Dasuqi pair has functioned for over two centuries as the standard reference for advanced Maliki legal study in North Africa and Egypt.

Ad-Dardiri's scholarly output extended beyond fiqh. He wrote on usul al-fiqh, the Arabic language, and Sufism. His combination of sober legal scholarship with authentic spiritual devotion made him a widely respected figure in his time and ensured that his works remained central to Azhari Maliki education long after his death.
title_id: Ad-Dardiri dan Al-Syarh al-Kabir: Otoritas Maliki di Mesir
content_id:
Ahmad ibn Muhammad al-'Adawi ad-Dardiri (1127-1201 H / 1715-1786 M) adalah di antara ulama Maliki paling terkemuka dari Mesir masa Ottoman. Lahir di Bani 'Adi di Mesir Hulu, ia pindah ke Kairo di mana ia belajar di masjid al-Azhar yang agung, naik melalui jenjang keilmuannya, dan akhirnya menjadi pengajar kepala (syekh) para ulama Maliki di sana. Ia dikenal bukan hanya karena keilmuan hukumnya tetapi karena kesalehannya dan afiliasinya dengan tarekat Sufi Khalwatiyyah.

Al-Syarh al-Kabir 'ala Mukhtashar Khalil (Komentar Besar atas Ringkasan Khalil) karya ad-Dardiri adalah magnum opusnya dalam fikih. Ini adalah komentar terperinci atas teks terpenting dalam tradisi Maliki: Mukhtashar Khalil ibn Ishaq al-Jundi (w. 776 H / 1374 M), sebuah ringkasan hukum Maliki yang padat yang begitu otoritatif sehingga menjadi referensi fondasi untuk fatwa dan keputusan yudisial Maliki di Afrika Utara, Afrika Barat, dan Mesir selama berabad-abad.

Mukhtashar Khalil sendiri adalah teks yang terkenal padat — memang disengaja demikian, karena dirancang untuk mengemas sejumlah besar informasi hukum dalam bentuk kompak yang cocok untuk dihafal oleh mahasiswa tingkat lanjut. Ia mengasumsikan keakraban dengan tradisi Maliki dan tidak dapat dipahami tanpa komentar. Al-Syarh al-Kabir ad-Dardiri mengisi peran ini, menyediakan penjelasan-penjelasan, kualifikasi-kualifikasi, dan pemahaman kontekstual yang diperlukan untuk membuat teks tersebut dapat digunakan.

Mazhab Maliki, yang didirikan oleh Imam Malik ibn Anas (93-179 H / 711-795 M) dari Madinah, adalah salah satu dari empat mazhab yurisprudensi Sunni yang diterima. Fitur-fitur metodologis yang khasnya meliputi: penghargaan tinggi terhadap praktik penduduk Madinah sebagai bukti Sunnah yang hidup yang ditransmisikan melalui praktik komunitas; kesediaan untuk menggunakan maslahah mursalah (kepentingan umum yang tidak terbatas) sebagai sumber hukum; dan fleksibilitas yang cukup besar dalam penerapan adat ('urf) sebagai faktor dalam hukum-hukum fikih.

Al-Syarh al-Kabir ada dalam hubungan yang komplementer dengan Hasyiyat al-Dasuqi 'ala asy-Syarh al-Kabir yang sama terkenal — sebuah super-komentar yang ditulis oleh murid ad-Dardiri, Muhammad ibn Ahmad al-Dasuqi. Kedua karya tersebut bersama-sama membentuk unit keilmuan yang tidak terpisahkan: hampir setiap mahasiswa fikih Maliki yang serius membacanya bersama-sama, karena catatan-catatan al-Dasuqi mengklarifikasi, memperluas, dan kadang mengoreksi posisi-posisi ad-Dardiri. Pasangan Dardiri-Dasuqi ini telah berfungsi selama lebih dari dua abad sebagai referensi standar untuk studi hukum Maliki tingkat lanjut di Afrika Utara dan Mesir.
content_ar: أحمد ibn محمد ﷺ al-'Adawi ad-Dardiri (1127–1201 AH / 1715–1786 CE) was among the most distinguished المالكي العلماء of Ottoman Egypt. Born in Bani 'Adi in Upper Egypt, he moved to Cairo where he studied at the great al-Azhar المسجد, rose through its العلمي ranks, and ultimately became the chief teacher (shaykh) of the المالكي العلماء there. He was known not only for his legal scholarship but for his piety and his affiliation with the Khalwatiyyah Sufi order — a combination of qualities that gave him authority in both the exoteric sciences of الفقه and the inner dimensions of Islamic spiritual practice. Ad-Dardiri's Al-Sharh al-Kabir ala Mukhtasar Khalil (The Great Commentary on the Summary of Khalil) is his magnum opus in الفقه. It is a detailed commentary on the single most المهم text in the المالكي التراث: the Mukhtasar of Khalil ibn Ishaq al-Jundi (d. 776 AH / 1374 CE), a compressed summary of المالكي law لذا المعتمد that it became the foundational reference for المالكي الفتوى and judicial decisions across North Africa, West Africa, and Egypt for centuries. The Mukhtasar Khalil is itself a notoriously dense text — deliberately لذا, as it is designed to pack an enormous quantity of legal information into a compact form suitable for memorization by advanced students. It assumes familiarity with the المالكي التراث and is unintelligible without commentary. Ad-Dardiri's Al-Sharh al-Kabir fills this role, providing the explanations, qualifications, and contextual الفهم necessary to make the text usable. The المالكي school, founded by الإمام مالك ibn أنس (93–179 AH / 711–795 CE) of المدينة المنورة, is one of the four accepted schools of Sunni jurisprudence. Its distinctive methodological features include: a high regard for the practice of the people of المدينة المنورة ('amal ahl al-المدينة المنورة) as evidence of the living السنة transmitted through الجماعة practice; a willingness to use maslaha mursalah (unrestricted public interest) as a source of law; the doctrine of sadd adh-dhara'i (blocking the means to harm); and considerable flexibility in the application of custom ('urf) as a factor in legal rulings. Egypt was historically a stronghold of both the الشافعي and المالكي schools, and ad-Dardiri's career at al-Azhar reflects the المالكي school's continued prominence in Egyptian العلمي الحياة خلال the Ottoman period. His commentary on Mukhtasar Khalil became one of the most consulted references in Egyptian and North African المالكي courts, used by judges (qadis) and muftis to resolve disputes and issue religious opinions. Al-Sharh al-Kabir exists in a complementary relationship with the equally famous Hashiyat al-Dasuqi ala ash-Sharh al-Kabir — a supercommentary written by ad-Dardiri's student محمد ﷺ ibn أحمد al-Dasuqi.
